title: Filter rows with logical conditions
text: dplyr::filter keeps rows where conditions are TRUE. It is commonly used for data cleaning, removing missing values with !is.na(x), and retaining valid numeric ranges such as price > 0. Multiple conditions are combined with AND semantics.

title: Create or transform columns
text: dplyr::mutate adds new variables or changes existing variables. A common sales workflow creates revenue = price * qty before grouping and summarising. Newly created columns can be used by later expressions in the same pipeline.

title: Group data before summaries
text: dplyr::group_by defines grouping variables such as store and month. Verbs like summarise then operate within each group. Grouping is useful for grouped totals, counts, and per-category statistics.

title: Summarise grouped data
text: dplyr::summarise returns one row per group. Typical summaries include sum(revenue, na.rm = TRUE), n(), mean(), and other aggregations. The .groups argument can be set to "drop" to return an ungrouped data frame.

title: Order rows
text: dplyr::arrange orders rows by one or more variables. Use desc(total_revenue) to sort grouped summaries from largest to smallest. Arrange usually appears near the end of a data pipeline.

title: Initialize a ggplot object
text: ggplot2::ggplot initializes a plot with a data frame and aesthetic mapping. Layers are added with +, and each geom can inherit or override the main mapping. The mpg data set is often used for examples.

title: Map variables to aesthetics
text: ggplot2::aes maps data variables to plot aesthetics such as x, y, color, shape, and size. For mpg, aes(x = displ, y = hwy, color = class) maps engine displacement, highway mileage, and vehicle class.

title: Draw scatter plots
text: ggplot2::geom_point creates scatter plots. It is appropriate for showing the relationship between two continuous variables such as displ and hwy, with optional color mapping for groups like class.

title: Create small multiples
text: ggplot2::facet_wrap creates a panel for each level of a faceting variable. facet_wrap(~ drv) is a common way to compare the same scatter plot across drivetrain categories in mpg.

title: Add labels to a plot
text: ggplot2::labs sets title, subtitle, axis labels, captions, and legend titles. Clear labels make generated visualization code easier to inspect and reuse.

title: Fit linear mixed-effects models
text: lme4::lmer fits linear mixed-effects models. The response and fixed effects are written like ordinary formulas, and random-effects terms are included in parentheses with a grouping factor, for example (Days | Subject).

title: Random effects formula syntax
text: In lme4 formula syntax, (1 | group) represents a random intercept, (x | group) represents random intercepts and random slopes for x by group, and (0 + x | group) represents random slopes without a random intercept. Fixed effects describe population-level trends.

title: Interpreting fixed and random effects
text: Fixed effects estimate average relationships across the full data set. Random effects estimate group-specific deviations, such as subject-level intercepts and slopes. Mixed models are useful when observations are clustered or repeated.

title: sleepstudy mixed model example
text: The lme4 sleepstudy example models Reaction as a function of Days with random effects by Subject. A common model is lmer(Reaction ~ Days + (Days | Subject), data = sleepstudy), where Days is the fixed effect and Subject has subject-specific intercepts and slopes.
